agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7 years. Framed as infrastructure optimization but intersects critically with EU/UK regulatory compliance, B2B contractual obligations, and consumer rights. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business policy change with legal exposure.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Retention","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: pickup, transit, proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient details, and any associated claims or disputes."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The business and legal rules governing how long delivery records must be preserved before deletion or anonymization."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signature, photo, GPS coordinates, timestamp. Critical for dispute resolution and compliance."},{"term":"Statute of Limitations Period","definition":"The legal window during which a claim (contractual, consumer, or tortious) can be brought. Varies by jurisdiction but typically 5-6 years in UK/EU."},{"term":"GDPR Data Minimization","definition":"GDPR principle requiring that personal data is kept no longer than necessary for its purpose. Does NOT mandate short retention — it requires justified retention."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations — often requiring multi-year data access."},{"term":"Customs Documentation","definition":"Import/export records tied to delivery that must be retained per EU customs regulations (typically 3-7 years depending on member state)."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The documented trail of who handled a shipment at each stage — critical for insurance claims, liability disputes, and regulatory audits."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for at least 6 years to cover the UK Limitation Act 1980 statute of limitations for contractual claims (6 years from breach).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a policy preference. If a customer or partner brings a contractual claim in year 5 and the data has been deleted, the company cannot defend itself. This creates direct legal liability.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record shall be deleted before 6 years from the delivery completion date in UK-market transactions."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records involving cross-border EU shipments must be retained per EU Customs Code Article 51 — minimum 3 years, but member states may extend to 10 years.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customs authorities can audit import/export records. Deletion before the statutory minimum makes the company non-compliant and subject to penalties.","testable_assertion":"Cross-border delivery records shall not be deleted before the applicable customs retention period for the relevant member state(s)."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Proof of Delivery records must be retained for the full dispute resolution window, which extends beyond delivery completion by the applicable statute of limitations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"POD is the primary evidence in delivery disputes. Without it, every disputed delivery defaults to a loss for the platform — financially and reputationally.","testable_assertion":"POD data shall exist for any delivery that is within its statute of limitations period."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the contractually agreed period, as B2B customers rely on this data for their own compliance reporting (tax, audit, customs).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Premature deletion breaches the data processing agreement and service contract with B2B customers, creating contractual liability and customer churn.","testable_assertion":"No B2B delivery record shall be deleted before the retention period specified in the applicable B2B service agreement."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR does not mandate 90-day deletion. It requires a lawful basis and proportionate retention period. Legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) and legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) justify multi-year retention of delivery records.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"GDPR interpretation is a policy decision informed by legal counsel, not a fixed physical constraint. However, the current framing that GDPR forces short retention is factually incorrect.","testable_assertion":"The data retention policy shall document a lawful basis under GDPR for the chosen retention period, reviewed by legal counsel."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Tax-relevant delivery records (VAT, duties) must be retained per HMRC requirements (6 years) and equivalent EU member state tax authorities.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit up to 6 years back (longer in fraud cases). Missing records during a tax audit results in estimated assessments and penalties.","testable_assertion":"Delivery records with tax implications (VAT, customs duties) shall be retained for a minimum of 6 years from the end of the relevant tax period."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A consumer files a breach of contract claim 4 years after delivery. The delivery record was deleted at 90 days.","expected_behavior":"The company has no evidence to defend the claim. Likely default judgment or costly settlement. This scenario is inevitable at scale."},{"scenario":"HMRC audits VAT on cross-border deliveries from 3 years ago. Records have been deleted.","expected_behavior":"Unable to substantiate VAT claims. HMRC issues estimated assessment with penalties. Potential investigation for non-compliance."},{"scenario":"A B2B logistics customer needs delivery data from 8 months ago for their annual compliance audit.","expected_behavior":"Data is gone. Customer's compliance audit fails. Breach of service agreement. Customer churns and potentially sues."},{"scenario":"An insurance claim for a high-value lost shipment is filed 6 months after delivery. Chain of custody records deleted at 90 days.","expected_behavior":"Cannot prove chain of custody. Insurance claim denied or platform bears full liability."},{"scenario":"EU customs authority audits cross-border shipment records from 2 years ago.","expected_behavior":"Records deleted. Customs penalties, potential suspension of customs privileges, impact on all cross-border operations."},{"scenario":"A consumer exercises GDPR right of access (SAR) for a delivery from 60 days ago, then another consumer for 120 days ago.","expected_behavior":"First request fulfilled. Second request — data already deleted. While deletion itself isn't a GDPR violation, if the lawful basis for processing required longer retention, premature deletion could indicate inadequate data governance."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Direct exposure to: (1) Undefendable litigation claims — at scale, even a small percentage of disputed deliveries over 90 days old creates significant liability. (2) Tax authority penalties — HMRC and EU tax authorities routinely audit 3-6 years back. (3) B2B contract breach claims and customer churn. (4) Insurance claim losses from missing chain of custody. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single adverse tax audit or class action. Estimated risk: 6-7 figures annually in a mature EU/UK operation vs. low-5-figure storage savings.","operational":"CRITICAL. (1) Customer support cannot resolve disputes older than 90 days — expect escalation volume spike. (2) B2B customers lose access to compliance data, generating support burden and contract renegotiations. (3) Legal team loses ability to respond to claims, increasing external legal costs. (4) Finance team cannot support tax audits.","user":"HIGH IMPACT. B2C consumers lose access to delivery history after 90 days — breaks expectations for purchase/delivery records. B2B customers lose critical compliance data, potentially causing them to fail their own regulatory obligations. Trust erosion across both segments.","risk":"EXTREME. This change creates regulatory non-compliance across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously. The risk is not theoretical — tax audits, customs audits, and contractual disputes are routine events in logistics. The 7-year retention exists for well-established legal reasons. Reducing to 90 days eliminates the company's ability to meet its legal obligations in the UK and EU.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Cannot fulfill their own compliance, tax, and audit obligations. Contractual breach by platform. High churn risk and potential litigation."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Lose delivery history and proof of delivery. Cannot reference past orders for disputes, returns, or warranty claims."},{"group":"Legal & Compliance Team","impact":"Unable to defend litigation claims. Unable to respond to regulatory inquiries. Professional liability concerns."},{"group":"Finance & Tax Team","impact":"Cannot support VAT audits, customs declarations, or financial reconciliation beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Cannot investigate or resolve any delivery issue older than 90 days. Increased escalations with no resolution path."},{"group":"Insurance & Risk","impact":"Cannot substantiate or contest insurance claims for shipments older than 90 days."},{"group":"Regulatory Bodies (HMRC, EU Customs, DPAs)","impact":"Company becomes non-compliant with retention obligations. Triggers enforcement risk."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the proposal as stated is fundamentally incompatible with known EU/UK legal requirements. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect — this is a business-critical policy change with regulatory implications. Cannot define valid acceptance criteria for a change that would make the platform non-compliant. The proposal needs to be rejected or fundamentally redesigned.","high_confidence_areas":["UK Limitation Act requires 6-year retention for contractual claims","HMRC requires 6-year VAT record retention","B2B customers contractually depend on multi-year data access","GDPR does not mandate 90-day deletion"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact customs retention requirements per EU member state (varies 3-10 years)","Specific B2B contractual retention commitments (need contract review)","Whether any delivery data categories could safely have shorter retention"],"assumptions":["The platform processes deliveries that cross EU member state borders","B2B service agreements include data availability commitments","Standard UK/EU regulatory requirements apply without special exemptions"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes' but 90-day retention directly contradicts UK Limitation Act (6 years), HMRC VAT requirements (6 years), EU Customs Code (3-10 years), and likely B2B contractual obligations. This is not an infrastructure decision — it is a compliance decision that requires legal review before any implementation begins.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal & Compliance","Finance & Tax","B2B Customers","Regulatory Bodies"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Implement tiered retention — anonymize PII after a defined period while retaining non-PII delivery records for the full legally required duration","C: Implement data archival (cold storage) instead of deletion to reduce costs while maintaining compliance","D: Engage legal counsel to define minimum retention per data category and jurisdiction, then implement category-specific policies"],"recommendation":"Option D first (legal review), then likely Option C (cold storage archival) combined with Option B (tiered anonymization). Storage cost reduction is achievable WITHOUT deletion through archival to cold storage (e.g., S3 Glacier), which typically reduces costs by 80-90% without creating legal exposure. This should never have reached engineering without legal sign-off."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion violates UK Limitation Act 1980 which requires ability to evidence contractual performance for 6 years","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion violates HMRC VAT record-keeping requirements (6 years)","business_rule_broken":"BR-6"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion violates EU Customs Code retention requirements (3-10 years)","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion likely breaches B2B service agreements requiring multi-year data availability","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Proof of Delivery destruction at 90 days eliminates ability to defend delivery disputes for the remaining 5+ years of the limitation period","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Change is mischaracterized as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance posture","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4, BR-6"}]}

This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business policy change with legal exposure.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Retention","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":
